data.title: ROBO: A Different Vision To Understand Its True Nature
data.description:
It is not the first time I talk about Robo Global® Robotics and Automation Index ETF (ROBO). And in the latest research, I started from a strong assumption, “if I look for competitive alternatives to the tech or AI sector, ROBO is not suitable”. In the end, if compared to this category, it is neither a source of de-correlation (beta), nor of added value in terms of returns (alpha). So the question is: “why have it in the portfolio?”. My answer is that ROBO, in my opinion, is one of the few ETFs truly vertical on the theme of robotics, which must be compared to instruments different from those commonly considered peers. Now I will explain why, but first.

A quick TL;DR

Compared to my latest coverage, here are some key elements to remember in order to understand what we are talking about when discussing ROBO:

  • ROBO is the first ETF in the world dedicated to robotics and automation, and it does so by adopting a physical replication of the ROBO Global Robotics and Automation Index.
  • This passive replication process involves costs that, in my opinion, are very high even for a thematic ETF: there is an Expense Ratio of 0.95% and a bid/ask spread of 0.24%. Even if the premium/discount is practically zero, and there is excellent tracking of the index, a 36% turnover remains. Summarily, this means an annual weight on performance that should not be underestimated.

The strategy of the index

At the head of the “ROBO Global Industry Classification” is the task of selecting the investable universe. Then it applies a filter based on qualitative criteria aimed at assigning a score ranging from 1 to 100. Apparently, only companies with a score above 50 are considered. This is associated with a semi-discretionary process on ROBO, because the committee seems to also have the ability to arbitrarily modify the methodology. And it is a distinctive trait that gives the ETF in question a data-driven touch that I personally appreciate.

Holding Distribution

  • The result is 77 companies, well distributed. Just think that the company with the highest weight inside the index is Symbotic, which accounts for about 2.42%, and the top 10 weighs about 19.7%.
  • This results in a distinctive composition, also at the geographical level: USA 44%, Asia 33%, Europe 23%. Specifically, Japan is overweighted, and already in the top 10 we find 3 holdings to keep in mind, Fanuc, Harmonic Drive, Yaskawa. It is indeed important to remember that 33% of the ETF is in Asia, well above the average of global tech ETFs.
  • And it also has distinctive traits in the market cap mix, composed of Large 51%, Mid 35%, Small 14%. It is one of the very few ETFs that does not have concentration on mega-cap.

Peer

A quick comparison will help to clarify ideas on the matter. In this regard, here is a peer table, which I personally consider quite exhaustive of the ETF market on the robotics theme:

It is impossible not to notice the fact that ROBO is the worst performer among the selected peers. But where is the reason? The reasons, in reality, are different:

  • The cost: among the ETFs considered, it is the one with the highest expenses. This ends up weighing directly on returns, especially when analyzing longer periods.

  • The benchmark: unlike products such as BOTZ, ARKQ, WTAI or THNQ, the other ETFs include a more significant share of companies linked to the artificial intelligence sector, which in recent years has driven much of the growth of the segment. It should be emphasized that ROBO instead is oriented 100% toward the global robotics sector. As a result, it has not benefited from the stock market boost of the hyperscalers post-2022; although it has still been significantly influenced by it. I’ll give you a hint: BOTZ has NVDA in the top 10 for 11%, ARKQ instead has TSLA for 12%, WTAI has the mag7 in the top 10, THNQ is already more diversified, but inside it has names like Alphabet.

Portfolio inclusion

2025 is an exceptional year, +23% YTD, above the ETF’s historical average, a signal that the robotics/automation cycle has entered a period of strong momentum after years of consolidation. The reason? the AI mega trend has also benefited the robotics trend. This is because the increase in CAPEX by AI hyperscalers should sooner or later generate some propulsive effect also outside the inner circle (what I like to call the “OpenAI family”). And the application that immediately comes to mind is robotics: but inside ROBO what is robotics? In the latest coverage, I presented a parallel that in my opinion helps a lot to understand what we are talking about when we talk about robotics in a stock market context: the correct comparison takes place in my opinion with BUYZ (ETF of disruptive commerce) or XLY (ETF of consumer discretionary).

All three show a similar trend, a sign that they are presumably driven by the same drivers, which in this specific case is discretionary or disruptive spending: therefore, in a certain sense, the economic trend.

But it is normal: automation, whether corporate or retail, is actually driven by the same drivers that basically guide XLY or BUYZ.

Expanding the time horizon, it becomes clear how in expansionary cycles, ROBO tends to outperform XLY. So this makes ROBO, in my opinion, a competitive ETF when compared to the right peers. If instead it is evaluated as a substitute for a technological ETF like QQQ or simply for solutions that also include AI inside, naturally a different result emerges.

Risk

It must be said that unlike XLY, which is a diversified ETF on the consumer discretionary theme, ROBO is vertical on the robotics segment. Although it is an expanding sector, it will naturally share the specific risks of the evolution of the robotics segment, and considering that AI has acted as a propulsive driver for 3Y growth, the evolutions of the AI segment will also have an influence on its quotations.

Conclusion

To conclude, I think that ROBO can prove to be a good portfolio ally, as a tactical or satellite component. If compared to other ETFs on the theme of “robotics and AI”, it remains a less competitive solution, but in my opinion it would be a misjudgment to make this comparison. The drivers that stimulate ROBO’s holdings are indeed AI, but also discretionality and disruptiveness in consumption, typical of ETFs like BUYZ and XLY.

data.title: Mastercard: A Premium Compounder You Don't Want To Underweight
data.description:
Quality Franchise With A Surprisingly Quiet Share Price

The way Mastercard Inc. (NYSE: MA) has been trading lately, I think, is more muted than it should be for an asset of this quality. I’m talking about a certain disconnect across different time frames this year, since over the last month it has fallen more than the index, approx. -5% vs -1%, and YTD it is up less than the index, 4% vs 16%.

I decided to take my time to digest the results and wait for the “noise” around them to settle, and for me, this Q3 looks like a solid set of numbers, something that shows up in revenue growing 17%, with cross-border and VAS (value added services) both running strong.I can already say that my underlying stance on MA, taking into account its structural double-digit growth, margins that are practically hovering around 60%, and the cash generation that allows the company to keep funding buybacks, is to assign a buy rating despite the recent pause in the share price. Although of course, I will go into detail in the following sections to justify that stance.

Volumes, Cross-Border And VAS Doing The Heavy Lifting

To continue putting the last quarter’s results into context, I start at the operating level, where revenue came in at around $8.6B (17% YoY), which also represents about 16% growth over the first nine months of 2025. For me, this confirms that the company is still accelerating, and that growth is being supported, in addition to higher card spending, by a mix that is increasingly diversified into services and international volumes that deliver more return per transaction.

Continuing with more data, gross dollar volume (GDV) reached $2.7T (+9% YoY), processed transactions came in at 45.4B (+10% YoY), and cross-border volume grew around +15% YoY in constant currency, mainly supported by travel and cross-border e-commerce. The way I read these numbers is that cards are being used more, and that, in addition, a growing part of the business is coming from categories such as tourism and international spending, with higher ticket sizes and better yield for the company. For me this is an important point, because the business is increasingly exposed to premium spending flows.

Even so, I also find the VAS piece interesting (25% YoY) reaching about $3.4B and representing almost 39% of quarterly sales. Why do I think this is interesting? Because, in my opinion, this reinforces my view that Mastercard is diversifying its business so it does not depend solely on the card network, adding data services, cybersecurity and different solutions for merchants.

However, it has not all been tailwinds, there are also several aspects that have triggered my radar and are worth mentioning. Incentives and rebates grew to about $3.5B (16% YoY), while operating expenses increased by a little over 15%. What triggers my radar here is that, although the business is growing, part of that growth is being directed to these better economic terms for large issuers, which means a higher cost base that limits how much of this advance ultimately reaches the bottom line. Although it is true that bank portfolios remain highly coveted targets, and that explains the competition and the resulting increase in costs.

So at the operating level, and despite the uptick in rebates and incentives and operating expenses, Mastercard continues to gain volume and diversify its services, which means that, even if this is not a quarter completely free of noise, the operational results lead me to think that the market may be undervaluing the company.

Still A Cash Machine

At the profitability level, after adjustments to gains on equity investments and a specific legal provision this quarter, both GAAP EPS and adjusted EPS change very little ($4.34 vs $4.38), which indicates that the results come from the quality of the business rather than being driven by adjustments.

It is also interesting to see, from my perspective as an investor, how much actually lands in cash, since in the first nine months of 2025 operating cash flow reaches $12.6B (+27% YoY), and if we take the last twelve months (TTM), we get CFO of around $17.5B, versus TTM net income of about $14B, so we have a cash conversion of earnings above 100%. For me this is due to a combination of high EBITDA with moderate depreciation, but above all to a model that barely needs working capital because it does not finance inventories.

You can see what I mean in the evolution of deferred revenue, which goes from around $459M at the end of 2023 to about $1.2B in this last Q3. These are revenues that will be recognized as the underlying obligations are fulfilled. So basically, using operating cash flow over the last twelve months, and adjusting it for these structural working capital movements, I see OCF consistently staying above net income.

Lastly, I have also looked at how intense investment really is, since if we add purchases of property and equipment, and capitalized software, we get TTM CapEx of around $1.1B. That is somewhat more stable than in previous years. And if we subtract this figure from TTM CFO of about $17.5B, “accounting” FCF would be close to $16B.

Again, we are looking at a cash machine, with FCF above 50% of sales and around 1.2x adjusted net income. On top of that, in this Q3 alone Mastercard has allocated about $678M to dividends, and around $3.3B to buybacks, so a meaningful part of this cash generation has been recycled back to shareholders without putting strain on the balance sheet. That is one of the reasons why I believe the premium multiple at which it trades is justified.

From Cash To Digital And New Payment Rails

Looking ahead, management indicates that Mastercard is heading into another year of low double-digit growth, while consensus is pricing in an EPS increase of over 20%, or about $19 per share for 2026.

From my point of view, I see both the guidance and the consensus as coherent considering that global GDP is expected to grow around 3%, with more contained inflation and, in addition, the company is projecting volumes and fees that run ahead of the economy.

And to support these statements, you can widen the focus to the structural catalysts that have driven Mastercard over the last decade, which in essence are still the same, but now sit on top of a larger business base, since there is now a non-cash consumer payments market that already moves between $35T and $40T, of which more than $6T are still in cash, so there is still room to be displaced.

In the end, the mere shift from cash to electronic adds several extra points of growth per year, especially in regions such as Latin America where the bulk of transactions are still in cash. But on the other hand, I also see initiatives to capture premium and high-ticket segments with Debit Mastercard in Europe, the PhonePe agreement in India, and the co-branded cards with Japan Airlines.

And finally, the network already allows people to buy and spend with crypto and stablecoins across its merchant base, with around 130 co-brand card programs linked to crypto, and more than 25% growth in crypto-related transactions in a single year. In addition, stablecoins are already being integrated into Mastercard Move for B2B use cases, so if the crypto ecosystem and tokenized payments do end up consolidating, part of that activity can be channeled through the same infrastructure the core business uses without having to reinvent a new network.

Premium Multiples, But A Risk-Reward That Still Looks Reasonable

My first impression is to see Mastercard as a quality company trading at demanding multiples, since with the share around $545 at the time of writing, a FWD P/E of about 33x and a TTM EV/EBITDA of around 25x, it is not exactly cheap, even if that normalizes to 30x and 22x respectively when you take 2025 estimates into account.

That said, I lean on this first point because we have in front of us a business that converts more than 50% of its sales into FCF, and with earnings growing at double digit rates, so my approach is not going to be to question whether it is “expensive” or trading at “high” multiples, but to check whether these multiples are coherent and can be sustained.

The FWD estimates I am using start from 2025 revenue of about $32.5B, and with a 2026 projection of:

Year Revenue EBITDA margin EBITDA Net margin Net income
2026e $36.8B 62% $22.8B 45% $16.5B

If I add to these figures the effect of buybacks taking the share count from 905M to around 880M by 2026, the estimated EPS should be around $18.5 to $19.5 per share. In line with consensus, which is around $19 per share, so the market seems to be pricing in that growth.

If I now run a comparison with its closest peers, these multiples can be analysed better:
